narrative: As an Asian male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 2.30, an LSAT score of 29.50, upper-middle family income, and full-time enrollment plans, along with your school decile rankings from Year 1 and Year 3 both sitting at the lowest 10th percentile—well below the typical student's mid-range performance around the 5th or 6th decile. The admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated an 81.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam, which directly led to your application's rejection as the school prioritizes candidates likely to pass and succeed in practice. This high failure probability means the model saw your profile as carrying substantial risk compared to applicants who typically show stronger academic trajectories. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking in the bottom 10%, as it signaled a concerning lack of improvement or consistency in grades relative to peers, far undercutting the average student's standing. Next in impact was your Year 1 decile, also at the bottom 10%, which reinforced the model's view of early academic struggles that often persist and hinder bar preparation. Somewhat counterbalancing this was your upper-middle family income, which nudged the prediction slightly toward success since students from similar backgrounds tend to have better bar passage rates on average, though it wasn't enough to offset the academic red flags. Overall, your metrics placed you below typical admitted students, whose LSATs average 36.70 and GPAs 3.21, amplifying the weight of those low deciles. While full-time status aligns with most applicants at 92.7%, it didn't sway the outcome here. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male with drive and background advantages—many overcome similar starts—but the model flags patterns from data where these academics correlate strongly with bar challenges. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly above average, strengthening your undergraduate record through a post-bac program or relevant experience, and targeting schools with holistic reviews that value your unique story. Reapplying after addressing these core areas could shift your predicted odds dramatically, opening doors to programs eager for diverse, determined students like you. Keep pushing; data-driven decisions evolve with improved inputs.
